words.shortDefinition: A multi-platform support library with a focus on asynchronous I/O.
words.longDefinition: The C library that provides the event loop and thread pool in Node.js. It handles asynchronous file system access, networking, and concurrency, allowing Node.js to be non-blocking even for operations that are synchronous at the OS level.
words.techContext: The 'engine room' of Node.js that manages the execution of asynchronous tasks behind the scenes.
words.exampleSentence: While JavaScript is single-threaded, libuv allows Node.js to perform multiple I/O operations in parallel using its internal thread pool.
words.etymology: from 'library' + 'UV' (Unicorn Velociraptor, the original project name).

words.shortDefinition: Objects for handling streaming data in Node.js.
words.longDefinition: An abstract interface for working with streaming data. Streams allow you to read data from a source or write data to a destination piece by piece, which is much more memory-efficient than loading the entire file into memory.
words.techContext: Essential for processing large files, handling network requests, or video streaming.
words.exampleSentence: By using streams, we were able to process a 4GB log file without ever exceeding 50MB of memory usage.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a continuous flow of water.

words.shortDefinition: Objects used to represent a fixed-size sequence of bytes.
words.longDefinition: The Buffer class in Node.js is designed to handle raw binary data. Since JavaScript's original String type was only for text, Buffers were introduced to work with TCP streams, file system operations, and other binary data sources.
words.techContext: Used whenever you're dealing with raw data that isn't necessarily text, such as image data or encrypted content.
words.exampleSentence: The file system module returns a Buffer when you read a file without specifying an encoding like 'utf8'.
words.etymology: from Middle French buffe ('a blow'), later meaning a temporary storage area in computing.

words.shortDefinition: A class that facilitates communication between objects in Node.js.
words.longDefinition: A core Node.js class that allows objects to emit named events that cause 'listeners' (functions) to be called. Much of the Node.js core (like HTTP servers and Streams) is built on top of EventEmitter.
words.techContext: The primary pattern for handling asynchronous events in Node.js, similar to 'addEventListener' in the browser.
words.exampleSentence: We created a custom Event Emitter to notify different parts of our app whenever a new user finishes the onboarding process.

words.shortDefinition: Functions that have access to the request and response objects in a web server.
words.longDefinition: Functions that execute during the lifecycle of a request to a Node.js server (usually Express). They can modify the request/response, end the request-response cycle, or call the next middleware in the stack.
words.techContext: Used for cross-cutting concerns like logging, authentication, parsing JSON, or handling errors.
words.exampleSentence: We added a middleware function to our Express app to verify the JWT token before allowing access to the protected API routes.

words.shortDefinition: A module that allows you to create child processes that share server ports.
words.longDefinition: Enables Node.js to take advantage of multi-core systems by spawning multiple 'worker' processes. Each worker runs its own instance of the Node.js event loop, but they all share the same network port.
words.techContext: Essential for scaling Node.js applications horizontally on a single machine to handle more traffic.
words.exampleSentence: By using the cluster module, we were able to scale our API to utilize all 16 cores on our production server.
words.etymology: from 'cluster' (a group of similar things positioned closely together).

words.shortDefinition: The original module system used in Node.js.
words.longDefinition: Uses 'require()' to import modules and 'module.exports' to export them. It is synchronous and was designed specifically for server-side use before official ES Modules existed.
words.techContext: While ES Modules are now standard, millions of NPM packages still use CommonJS, so Node.js developers must understand both.
words.exampleSentence: Even though our new code uses 'import', we still have to use 'require' for some of our older internal libraries that use CommonJS.
words.etymology: Named as a 'common' standard for JavaScript modules.

words.shortDefinition: The official standard for JavaScript modules.
words.longDefinition: Also known as ESM. Uses 'import' and 'export' statements. Unlike CommonJS, ESM is asynchronous and allows for features like top-level await and better tree-shaking.
words.techContext: The modern standard for both browser and server-side JavaScript, gradually replacing CommonJS in the Node.js ecosystem.
words.exampleSentence: We switched our Node.js project to ES Modules by adding 'type: module' to our package.json file.

words.shortDefinition: The manifest file for a Node.js project.
words.longDefinition: A JSON file that contains metadata about the project (name, version), defines its dependencies, and specifies scripts for common tasks like testing or building.
words.techContext: The 'heart' of every Node.js and NPM project. It ensures that everyone working on the project uses the same versions of libraries.
words.exampleSentence: I added the 'start' script to our package.json so anyone can run the app using 'npm start'.

words.shortDefinition: The default package manager for Node.js.
words.longDefinition: Node Package Manager. It consists of a command-line client and an online database (registry) of public and private packages. It is the largest software registry in the world.
words.techContext: Used to install, share, and manage dependencies in Node.js applications.
words.exampleSentence: I used NPM to install the latest version of Express and add it to our project's dependencies.

words.shortDefinition: A tool for executing NPM packages without installing them globally.
words.longDefinition: Included with NPM, npx allows you to run a command from an NPM package (like 'create-react-app' or 'prisma') without having to download and manage the package globally on your machine.
words.techContext: Great for one-off commands or ensuring you're always using the latest version of a CLI tool.
words.exampleSentence: Instead of installing the linter globally, I just use 'npx eslint' to check my code.

words.shortDefinition: A group of worker threads managed by libuv.
words.longDefinition: While JS is single-threaded, libuv maintains a pool of 4 (by default) background threads used for heavy operations like file I/O, DNS lookups, and cryptography that would otherwise block the main event loop.
words.techContext: You can increase the size of the thread pool using the 'UV_THREADPOOL_SIZE' environment variable for better performance in I/O-heavy apps.
words.exampleSentence: Our image processing was slow because we were saturating the default thread pool with too many simultaneous requests.

words.shortDefinition: A form of I/O processing that allows other tasks to continue while waiting for data.
words.longDefinition: In Node.js, I/O operations (like reading from a disk or a network) are asynchronous. The request is started, and Node continues to execute other code. When the data is ready, a callback is triggered.
words.techContext: The reason Node.js is highly scalable for network-intensive applications despite being single-threaded.
words.exampleSentence: Thanks to non-blocking I/O, our server can handle thousands of concurrent WebSocket connections without spawning a thread for each one.

words.shortDefinition: An interactive shell for executing JavaScript code.
words.longDefinition: Read-Eval-Print Loop. An interactive environment that takes single user inputs, executes them, and returns the result to the user. You can access Node's REPL by simply typing 'node' in your terminal.
words.techContext: Great for quickly testing small snippets of code or experimenting with Node.js APIs without creating a file.
words.exampleSentence: I jumped into the Node REPL to quickly check how the 'path.join' function handles relative directories.

words.shortDefinition: A global object that provides information about the current Node.js process.
words.longDefinition: Provides control over the current Node.js process. You can use it to get environment variables, listen for exit events, or read command-line arguments (process.argv).
words.techContext: A fundamental part of writing Node.js CLI tools and managing application state across different environments.
words.exampleSentence: We used the process object to listen for the 'SIGINT' signal so our server could shut down gracefully when someone presses Ctrl+C.

words.shortDefinition: Variables whose values are set outside the program, typically in the OS or a .env file.
words.longDefinition: Used to configure applications without changing the code. In Node.js, these are accessed via the 'process.env' object.
words.techContext: Essential for storing sensitive data like API keys, database passwords, and port numbers that change between development and production.
words.exampleSentence: Never hardcode your database password; always use an environment variable and access it via process.env.DB_PASS.

words.shortDefinition: The Node.js core module for interacting with the file system.
words.longDefinition: File System module. It provides APIs for reading, writing, deleting, and renaming files and directories. It offers both synchronous and asynchronous (callback, promise-based) methods.
words.techContext: The 'fs/promises' variant is the modern standard for writing clean, async/await file system code.
words.exampleSentence: I used the FS module to read the configuration file and parse it as JSON before the server started.

words.shortDefinition: The Node.js core module for handling and transforming file paths.
words.longDefinition: Provides utilities for working with file and directory paths. It handles the differences between Windows (backslashes) and POSIX (forward slashes) file systems automatically.
words.techContext: Essential for building cross-platform Node.js tools. Use 'path.join' instead of manual string concatenation to avoid bugs.
words.exampleSentence: Always use path.join to construct file paths so your application works correctly on both Windows and Linux.

words.shortDefinition: The process of connecting the output of one stream to the input of another.
words.longDefinition: A mechanism to take the data from a readable stream and 'pipe' it directly into a writable stream. This is highly efficient and handles data flow control (backpressure) automatically.
words.techContext: Commonly used to take an uploaded file and pipe it directly to a storage service or a compression stream.
words.exampleSentence: We piped the file stream directly into the Gzip stream and then into the response object to send a compressed file to the user.
words.etymology: Metaphor for connecting two physical pipes together.

words.shortDefinition: The problem where a data source produces data faster than a destination can consume it.
words.longDefinition: In Node.js streams, backpressure occurs when a writable stream can't keep up with the readable stream. Node's piping mechanism handles this by telling the readable stream to pause until the writable stream is ready for more.
words.techContext: Crucial for preventing memory overflows in high-speed data processing pipelines.
words.exampleSentence: Without proper backpressure handling, our server would have crashed because the database couldn't write the logs as fast as the network was receiving them.

words.shortDefinition: A versioning scheme for software that uses a three-part number: Major.Minor.Patch.
words.longDefinition: Also known as SemVer. Major version changes indicate breaking changes; Minor adds functionality in a backwards-compatible way; Patch is for backwards-compatible bug fixes.
words.techContext: The system used by NPM to determine which version of a package is safe to install when a range (like ^1.2.0) is specified.
words.exampleSentence: Since the new version of the library includes a breaking change to the API, we had to bump its major version according to SemVer.

words.shortDefinition: A situation where multiple nested callbacks make code hard to read and maintain.
words.longDefinition: Also known as the 'Pyramid of Doom'. It occurs when asynchronous operations are nested within each other, leading to code that grows horizontally rather than vertically.
words.techContext: The primary problem that led to the adoption of Promises and async/await in modern JavaScript.
words.exampleSentence: We refactored our legacy database logic to use async/await because the callback hell was making it impossible to debug.
words.etymology: Descriptive of the frustration caused by deeply nested code.

words.shortDefinition: The process of converting a callback-based function into one that returns a Promise.
words.longDefinition: Node.js provides a utility 'util.promisify' that takes a traditional error-first callback function and transforms it into a function that can be used with '.then()' or 'await'.
words.techContext: Used to modernize older Node.js APIs or third-party libraries that haven't been updated to use Promises natively.
words.exampleSentence: I used util.promisify on the legacy 'setTimeout' function so I could use it more easily inside my async functions.

words.shortDefinition: A coding pattern where the first argument of a callback is an error object.
words.longDefinition: The standard way Node.js handles asynchronous errors. If an error occurs, it is passed as the first argument; if not, the first argument is 'null' and the data follows as the second argument.
words.techContext: While async/await is now common, you will still see this pattern in many older Node.js core modules and third-party packages.
words.exampleSentence: Always check the first argument of your callback; if it's not null, something went wrong during the operation.

words.shortDefinition: An object that is available in all modules of a Node.js application.
words.longDefinition: The equivalent of 'window' in the browser. It contains built-in objects like Buffer, __dirname, and __filename, as well as timers like setTimeout.
words.techContext: While you can attach custom properties to 'global', it is generally considered an anti-pattern as it makes code harder to test and reason about.
words.exampleSentence: You don't need to 'require' the Buffer class because it's already part of the global object in Node.js.
words.etymology: from Latin globalis ('of the whole world').
